---
title: "1.3 From raw sequencing data to assemblies"
author: "Nguyen MTS"
date: "2026-05-12"
---

# Introduction

From this module to the end of Day-3, we will work on a public dataset of a nosocomial *K. pneumoniae* outbreak in Italy.

The dataset is under the BioProject ID: PRJNA1260529, with the publication by Captani et al, 2026 (https://doi.org/10.2807/1560-7917.ES.2026.31.10.2500457).

We will learn how to conduct analyses to obtain annotated genomes, with genes and their locations, as what we have explored in the previous module, from raw sequencing data.

Our workflow will comprise

# Data directory

If you have completed Day-0, you probably have downloaded the dataset in the last exercise. But if you haven't, no worries, you got we covered! The data is available in your instance, under the directories `/data/raw_shortread` and `/data/raw_longread`.

We will first focus on the short-read sequencing data. Long-read sequencing and how to analyse their output will be introduced in module 2.2.

```         
├── raw_shortread 

│   ├── 925Kp_1.fastq.gz 

│   ├── 925Kp_2.fastq.gz 

│   ├── 930Kp_1.fastq.gz 

│   ├── 930Kp_2.fastq.gz 

│   ├── ... 
```

# FASTQ files

Raw sequencing data are stored in FASTQ format, usually with tail `.fastq` or `.fq` in their filenames, or `.fastq.gz` / `.fq.gz` if they are compressed.

For paired-end short-reads, each sample or isolate will have 2 FASTQ files - one for forward, and one for reverse reads. In our dataset, the files are named as `{sampleID}_1.fastq.gz` and `{sampleID}_2.fastq.gz` for forward and reverse reads, respectively.

Each short-read FASTQ file contains the following information: - ... - ... - ...

# Reads Quality

Measuring the quality of the raw read files can be conducted with FastQC, and the reports from it can be synthesized by MultiQC.

The following command-lines call the two programs on our short-read data:

``` bash
in_dir="raw_shortread/"
fastqc_outdir="fastqc_shortread/"
mkdir -p $fastqc_outdir
multiqc_outdir="multiqc_shortread/"
mkdir -p $multiqc_outdir

conda activate multiqc
nthreads=5
fastqc -o $fastqc_outdir -t $nthreads $in_dir/*.fastq.gz
multiqc --interactive --force -o $multiqc_outdir $fastqc_outdir
```

# Assembly

``` bash
parent_outdir="unicycler_shortread"
mkdir -p $parent_outdir

assembly(){
    local sample="$1";
    local forward="$2";
    local reverse="$3";
    local parent_outdir="$4";
    local outdir="${parent_outdir}/${sample}";

    unicycler -1 $forward -2 $reverse -o $outdir --threads 4;
}
export -f assembly

conda activate tormes-1.3.0;

while read -r sample; do
    echo -e ${sample}"\t"raw_shortread/${sample}_1.fastq.gz"\t"raw_shortread/${sample}_2.fastq.gz >> samples_shortread.txt;
done < <(grep "Illumina MiSeq" PRJNA1260529.metadata.tsv | cut -f28)

parallel -j 5 --colsep "\t" assembly {1} {2} {3} $parent_outdir :::: samples_shortread.txt
```

# Self-Explore

## Improve reads quality for assembly

Trimming and adapter removal can be done with FASTP or Trimmomatic

Example command-lines:

## Is my isolate really a K. pneumoniae?

Species check can be done directly on the raw short-reads sequencing data, thanks to techniques from metagenomics.

``` bash
SYLPH_DB="sylph-db/gtdb-r220-c200-dbv1.syldb"
sylph profile $SYLPH_DB -1 raw_shortread/*_1.fastq.gz -2 raw_shortread/*_2.fastq.gz -o sylph_shortread.tsv
```
